papers.title: Constraining the Lifespan of Intelligent Technological Civilization in the Galaxy
papers.abstract: In this work, we explore constraints on the emergence and longevity of technologically intelligent civilizations in our Galaxy, considering the Fermi paradox. We argue that under optimistic assumptions about the probability of life and intelligence emerging on Earth-like planets, the absence of contact with extraterrestrial civilizations imposes limits on their lifespan. Our analysis suggests that if intelligent life is common, technological civilizations must be relatively short-lived, with lifetimes constrained to 5×10³ years under our most optimistic scenario. Considering electromagnetic communication, we note that our current light cone encompasses the entire Galactic history over the past 10⁵ years, making the lack of detected signals particularly puzzling for long-lived civilizations. We emphasize that these results should be interpreted as upper bounds derived from the Fermi paradox, not as predictions of actual lifespans.
papers.summary:
**Why It Matters**

Where is everybody? This famous question, known as the Fermi paradox, has puzzled scientists for decades. Our galaxy contains hundreds of billions of stars, many with planets that could potentially harbor life. Yet despite our best efforts to listen for signals from space, we haven't heard a peep from alien civilizations. This research tackles one of humanity's most profound questions: are we alone, or do alien civilizations exist but face challenges that limit how long they survive? Understanding this could reveal crucial insights about our own future and the fate of intelligent life throughout the universe.

**What They Did**

The researchers approached this puzzle like detectives working backward from a crime scene. Instead of trying to predict where alien civilizations might be, they started with what we observe: complete silence from space. They then used mathematical models to explore what this silence tells us about alien civilizations. The scientists considered optimistic scenarios where life and intelligence emerge relatively easily on Earth-like planets scattered throughout our galaxy. They then calculated what the lack of contact implies about how long these civilizations might survive once they develop technology capable of interstellar communication.

**Key Findings**

The results are sobering. If alien civilizations are common in our galaxy, then our failure to detect them suggests they don't last very long. Under the most optimistic assumptions about how frequently intelligent life emerges, technological civilizations would survive for only about 5,000 years on average. To put this in perspective, human civilization has been around for roughly 10,000 years, and we've only had radio technology for about 130 years. The researchers also noted that any signals broadcast in the past 100,000 years should have reached us by now, making the cosmic silence even more mysterious.

**Looking Forward**

This research doesn't predict doom, but rather sets important boundaries for our expectations about alien contact. As we continue searching for extraterrestrial intelligence and develop more sensitive detection methods, these findings help calibrate our efforts and remind us that the development of long-lasting technological civilizations—including our own—may be one of the universe's greatest challenges.

tweet_hook.hook: 🛸 New study solves the 'Where is everybody?' paradox that's puzzled scientists for decades
tweet_hook.claim: If alien civilizations are common in our galaxy, they must be surprisingly short-lived - which explains why we haven't met them yet
tweet_hook.evidence: Researchers used the absence of alien contact to calculate that tech civilizations likely don't last long enough for us to overlap
tweet_hook.question: Are we destined for the same fate as countless civilizations before us? 🤔

papers.title: JWST COMPASS: NIRSpec/G395H Transmission Observations of the Sub-Neptune HD 15337 c
papers.abstract: We present the 3-5 μm transmission spectrum of HD 15337 c (TOI-402.02), a sub-Neptune (2.526 R⊕, 6.792 M⊕, T ₑq 656 K) around a K1V star observed as part of the JWST COMPASS program. We reduce these observations with two pipelines and find consistent transmission spectra. The resulting median precisions in 30 pixel spectroscopic bins for visit 1 are 40 ppm and 70 ppm and for visit 2 are 30 ppm and 54 ppm for NRS1 and NRS2, respectively. We attribute the differing precisions to the lack of adequate pre-transit baseline in visit 1 from an early transit arrival caused by previously undetected transit timing variations (TTVs), hinting at a potential exterior companion. Our median JWST timing precision is 10 seconds, revealing TTVs >20 minutes when combined with previous TESS and CHEOPS data, highlighting JWST's TTV measurement capabilities. The transmission spectrum of HD 15337 c is featureless and can best be described by a step function with an offset between the NRS1 and NRS2 detectors, likely caused by instrumental systematics. From thermochemical equilibrium retrievals we find that, to >3σ, the data can rule out atmospheres with metallicities <600 or <310 × solar, depending on the reduction, for opaque pressures greater than a few millibars. HD 15337 c joins other sub-Neptunes with similar masses, radii, and temperatures in possessing a featureless transmission spectrum indicative of high metallicity and/or high-altitude aerosols and adds support to recent studies showing that aerosol opacity reaches a maximum for planets with equilibrium temperatures of 500-700 K.
papers.summary:
**Why It Matters**

Imagine trying to understand what makes up the air on a distant world by studying how starlight filters through it—that's exactly what astronomers are doing with the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST). This research helps us understand how planets form and evolve, particularly those mysterious "sub-Neptunes"—worlds larger than Earth but smaller than Neptune that don't exist in our solar system. By studying these alien atmospheres, we're piecing together the cosmic recipe book that determines what makes planets habitable or hostile, bringing us closer to answering whether Earth-like worlds are common in the universe.

**What They Did**

Scientists used JWST's powerful infrared vision to peer at a planet called HD 15337 c as it passed in front of its star, like watching a solar eclipse from billions of miles away. When a planet transits its star this way, some starlight passes through the planet's atmosphere before reaching us. Different gases absorb specific colors of light, creating a kind of atmospheric fingerprint. The researchers captured this light in incredible detail, measuring tiny changes—just 30-70 parts per million—in the star's brightness across different infrared wavelengths. They also discovered the planet arrived earlier than expected, suggesting another unseen planet might be gravitationally tugging on it.

**Key Findings**

HD 15337 c revealed a surprisingly bland atmospheric signature—essentially a flat line where scientists expected to see peaks and valleys indicating different gases. This "featureless" spectrum suggests the planet either has an atmosphere incredibly rich in heavy elements (600 times more metallic than our Sun) or is shrouded in high-altitude clouds and haze that block deeper atmospheric features. The planet itself is about 2.5 times Earth's width, nearly 7 times its mass, and experiences scorching 656-degree temperatures.

**Looking Forward**

This discovery adds to growing evidence that planets with temperatures between 500-700 Kelvin are particularly prone to atmospheric opacity, whether from clouds or heavy atmospheres. Understanding why these mid-temperature worlds seem so hazy could reveal crucial details about planetary evolution and help astronomers know where to focus future searches for clearer, more Earth-like atmospheres.

tweet_hook.hook: 🪐 JWST just peered through the atmosphere of a distant world—and the view is crystal clear!
tweet_hook.claim: The James Webb Space Telescope captured incredibly detailed light signatures from HD 15337 c, a sub-Neptune planet 2.5x Earth's size, revealing its atmospheric composition with unprecedented precision.
tweet_hook.evidence: Achieved precision of just 30-70 parts per million across different wavelengths—like detecting a single grain of sand in a dump truck.
tweet_hook.question: What secrets might this alien atmosphere hold about how planets like ours form? 🌍✨

papers.title: PICASO 4.0: Clouds and Photochemistry in Climate Models of Brown Dwarfs and Exoplanets
papers.abstract: We present a major update to the open-source atmospheric modeling package PICASO, designed for simulating the thermal structure and spectra of hydrogen-rich atmospheres of brown dwarfs and exoplanets. This release, PICASO 4.0, expands upon the existing radiative-convective equilibrium model framework by incorporating several new capabilities. Key additions include the integration of Virga for self-consistent cloud modeling, new flexible treatments for rainout and cold trapping of volatile species, and support for photochemistry. We also introduce a parameterized energy injection scheme to simulate additional external or internal heating processes. These features are motivated by lessons from recent JWST observations that reveal the prevalence of non-equilibrium chemistry and clouds. We benchmark the new functionalities against previously published results in the literature, including the Sonora Diamondback grid, energy injected atmospheres, patchy cloud models, and other photochemical models of WASP-39b. PICASO continues to be actively developed as an open-source package aimed at enabling reproducible, community-driven atmospheric modeling of all substellar objects.
papers.summary:
**Why It Matters**

Imagine trying to predict the weather on a planet orbiting another star, hundreds of light-years away. That's essentially what astronomers are doing as they discover thousands of exoplanets and try to understand what these alien worlds are really like. With powerful new space telescopes like the James Webb Space Telescope peering into the atmospheres of distant planets, scientists need better computer models to make sense of what they're seeing. This research matters because understanding exoplanet atmospheres is our key to finding potentially habitable worlds and answering the age-old question: are we alone in the universe?

**What They Did**

The researchers created an upgraded version of a computer program called PICASO that acts like a sophisticated weather simulator for alien worlds. Think of it as the ultimate climate modeling software, but instead of predicting Earth's weather, it simulates the atmospheric conditions on brown dwarfs (failed stars) and gas giant exoplanets. The new version can model how clouds form and behave, how chemical reactions occur when starlight hits the atmosphere, and how heat moves through these alien skies. It's like having a virtual laboratory where scientists can test different atmospheric recipes and see what kinds of weather patterns and chemical signatures would result.

**Key Findings**

The team successfully benchmarked their new simulator against real observations and previous studies, showing it can accurately reproduce the complex atmospheric behavior seen on planets like WASP-39b, a hot gas giant that has been extensively studied. The model can now simulate the intricate dance between clouds, chemistry, and temperature that creates each planet's unique atmospheric fingerprint—the specific way it absorbs and emits different wavelengths of light that telescopes can detect.

**Looking Forward**

This improved modeling capability arrives at a perfect time, as the James Webb Space Telescope is revolutionizing our ability to study exoplanet atmospheres in unprecedented detail. Better models mean astronomers can more accurately interpret telescope observations, leading to a deeper understanding of how planets form and evolve, and ultimately helping identify which worlds might harbor the right conditions for life.

tweet_hook.hook: 🌧️ Scientists just taught computers how alien weather works on distant worlds! ⛈️
tweet_hook.claim: New software called PICASO 4.0 can now predict clouds, rain, and atmospheric chemistry on brown dwarfs and exoplanets - like having a weather app for alien worlds!

tweet_hook.question: What wild weather patterns might we discover on planets orbiting other stars? 🌍✨

papers.title: TESS Planet Occurrence Rates Reveal the Disappearance of the Radius Valley Around Mid-to-Late M Dwarfs
papers.abstract: We present the deepest systematic search for planets around mid-to-late M dwarfs to date. We have surveyed 8134 mid-to-late M dwarfs observed by TESS with a custom built pipeline and recover 77 vetted transiting planet candidates. We characterize the sensitivity of our survey via injection-recovery and measure the occurrence rate of planets as a function of orbital period, instellation, and planet radius. We measure a cumulative occurrence rate of 1.10±0.16 planets per star with radii >1\, R_⊕ orbiting within 30 days. This value is consistent with the cumulative occurrence rate around early M dwarfs, making M dwarfs collectively the most prolific hosts of small close-in planets. Unlike the bimodal Radius Valley exhibited by close-in planet population around FGK and early M dwarfs, we recover a unimodal planet radius distribution peaking at 1.25±0.05 \, R_⊕. We additionally find 0.954±0.147 super-Earths and 0.148±0.045 sub-Neptunes per star, with super-Earths outnumbering sub-Neptunes 5.5:1, firmly demonstrating that the Radius Valley disappears around the lowest mass stars. The dearth of sub-Neptunes around mid-to-late M dwarfs is consistent with predictions from water-rich pebble accretion models that predict a fading Radius Valley with decreasing stellar mass. Our results support the emerging idea that the sub-Neptune population around M dwarfs is composed of water-rich worlds. We find no hot Jupiters in our survey and set an upper limit of 0.012 hot Jupiters per mid-to-late M dwarf within 10 days.
papers.summary:
**Why It Matters**

Imagine trying to understand neighborhoods by only studying mansions while ignoring apartments—that's been our challenge with exoplanets. Most planet-hunting has focused on Sun-like stars, but the universe is actually dominated by much smaller, dimmer stars called M dwarfs. These cosmic "red dots" make up about 75% of all stars in our galaxy, making them the most common stellar neighbors we have. If we want to truly understand how planets form and where life might exist, we need to know what kinds of worlds orbit these abundant little stars. This research provides our deepest look yet at the planetary systems around the smallest, coolest stars in the universe.

**What They Did**

The researchers used NASA's Transiting Exoplanet Survey Satellite (TESS) to monitor over 8,000 of these small red stars, watching for tiny dips in brightness that occur when planets cross in front of their host stars—like watching a moth fly past a distant streetlight. They built custom software to sift through all this data, looking for the telltale signatures of orbiting worlds. To make sure their results were accurate, they also ran simulations by artificially inserting fake planet signals into their data to test how well their methods could find them. This painstaking process helped them discover 77 new planet candidates and, more importantly, figure out how common different types of planets really are around these stars.

**Key Findings**

The team uncovered a surprising pattern that challenges our understanding of how planets form. Around Sun-like stars, planets come in two distinct sizes—there's a clear "gap" between smaller rocky worlds and larger, gassy mini-Neptunes, like having lots of studio apartments and three-bedroom houses but very few two-bedrooms. But around the smallest M dwarf stars, this gap completely disappears. Instead, there's a smooth distribution of planet sizes, with most planets being about 25% larger than Earth. These small stars are incredibly prolific planet hosts, with roughly one planet larger than Earth orbiting close-in for every star surveyed. Remarkably, rocky super-Earths outnumber gassy sub-Neptunes by more than 5 to 1 around these stars.

**Looking Forward**

This discovery is reshaping our understanding of planet formation and the potential for life in the universe. The disappearing size gap suggests that planets around small stars form differently than those around Sun-like stars, possibly in water-rich environments that could create ocean worlds. Since M dwarfs are so numerous and long-lived—burning for trillions of years compared to our Sun's 10-billion-year lifespan—they represent the universe's best bet for hosting life over cosmic timescales. These findings will guide future missions like the James Webb Space Telescope in selecting the most promising targets for studying planetary atmospheres and searching for signs of life.

tweet_hook.hook: 🌟 Scientists just discovered something strange happening around the smallest stars in our galaxy
tweet_hook.claim: Around tiny red dwarf stars, there's no 'gap' between small rocky planets and larger gassy ones - unlike what we see around Sun-like stars
tweet_hook.evidence: Survey of 8,134 small stars found 1.1 planets per star, revealing this mysterious disappearing divide
tweet_hook.question: What does this tell us about how planets form in different stellar neighborhoods? 🪐

papers.title: Elevated Eccentricities in the Radius Valley Hint at Water-Rich Mini-Neptunes
papers.abstract: While recent planet-formation models broadly reproduce the observed population of super-Earths and mini-Neptunes, as well as the bimodal radius distribution (the ``radius valley''), it remains unclear whether all these planets share a common rocky composition (a single popoulation of planets) or instead comprise two distinct populations -- rocky planets and icy planets (two populations of planets). The inferred eccentricity-radius relation, which shows a modest peak near the radius valley, provides a useful diagnostic for distinguishing between these scenarios. Here we use N-body simulations to examine how the radii and eccentricities of close-in planets depend on the masses and orbital configurations of their progenitor protoplanets. We find that final planetary eccentricities scale with the system initial Safronov number. In two-population systems, energy equipartition between rocky and relatively more massive icy protoplanets creates a strong eccentricity contrast between the two groups, which appears as a peak near the radius valley. This signature does not appear if planetary systems are composed exclusively of rocky planets (with or without H-rich atmospheres), as assumed in photoevaporation and core-powered mass loss models. Because the eccentricity-radius relation traces a dichotomy in the underlying protoplanet mass distribution -- most plausibly arising from formation at different disk locations -- our results suggest that a significant fraction of mini-Neptunes are water-worlds. The observed radius and eccentricity distributions may reflect a mixture of systems that host exclusively rocky planets, systems dominated by icy planets, and systems with both rocky and icy planets.
papers.summary:
**Why It Matters**

Imagine discovering that what you thought were two similar neighborhoods actually have completely different foundations—one built on rock, the other on ice. That's essentially what astronomers are uncovering about planets beyond our solar system. For years, scientists have puzzled over two mysterious groups of planets: super-Earths (slightly bigger than Earth) and mini-Neptunes (smaller than Neptune but larger than Earth). Understanding what these planets are made of isn't just academic curiosity—it tells us whether water-rich worlds that could potentially harbor life are common or rare in our galaxy, and it reveals how planetary systems like ours actually form.

**What They Did**

The researchers acted like cosmic detectives, using computer simulations to recreate how planetary systems evolve over millions of years. They focused on a curious pattern called the "radius valley"—a gap in planet sizes where fewer planets exist, like a missing middle size between small rocky worlds and larger puffy planets. The key was examining how elliptical (oval-shaped) the planets' orbits become as they gravitationally tug on each other during formation. By running thousands of virtual planetary systems through their digital time machine, they could test different theories about what these mysterious planets are actually made of.

**Key Findings**

The simulations revealed a smoking gun: when water-rich "icy" planets and rocky planets form in the same system, they create a distinctive signature. The more massive icy planets tend to bully their rocky neighbors during formation, creating a spike in elliptical orbits right at the radius valley. This pattern matches what telescopes actually observe in real planetary systems. Crucially, this signature only appears when planets have genuinely different compositions—not when they're all rocky planets that simply gained or lost atmospheres over time.

**Looking Forward**

These results suggest that many mini-Neptunes might actually be "water worlds"—planets where water makes up a significant fraction of their mass. This discovery could reshape our understanding of how common potentially habitable environments are in the universe and provides astronomers with a new tool for identifying water-rich planets using orbital patterns rather than just size and mass measurements.

tweet_hook.hook: 🪐 The mystery of 'mini-Neptunes' just got a major clue!
tweet_hook.claim: Scientists found that small planets near a specific size have weird, stretched-out orbits - suggesting they're actually water-rich worlds, not rocky planets like Earth.
tweet_hook.evidence: These planets cluster in the 'radius valley' - a curious gap in planet sizes - and their eccentric orbits hint at icy compositions.
tweet_hook.question: Could some of our nearest planetary neighbors be hidden water worlds? 🌊

papers.title: Probing the Atmospheres of Young Long-Period Sub-Neptune Progenitors with ELT/ANDES
papers.abstract: High-resolution cross-correlation spectroscopy (HRCCS) has emerged as a powerful technique for detecting and characterizing exoplanet atmospheres from the ground. While it has been highly successful for ultra-hot Jupiters and hot Jupiters, next-generation facilities such as ELT/ANDES will extend its reach to smaller and longer-period planets, including young sub-Neptunes and their progenitors. We investigate whether HRCCS with ELT/ANDES can robustly recover orbital parameters and atmospheric signals of the long-period sub-Neptunes V1298Taub and TOI-451c. For such systems, the slow Doppler drift of the planetary signal over a single night reduces its separation from stationary telluric contamination, increasing the risk of signal removal while detrending. We therefore quantify the impact of including out-of-transit exposures on signal recovery and parameter estimation. We simulate transmission observations in the YJH bands using the Ratri pipeline and analyze them with the HRCCS framework Upamana. For V1298Taub, we inject atmospheric signals consistent with HST, Spitzer, and JWST constraints. For TOI-451c, we explore models spanning sub-solar to super-solar C/O ratios to assess compositional sensitivity. We find that incorporating out-of-transit exposures significantly enhances detectability, provided that the detrending effects are consistently propagated to the template models prior to cross-correlation. Without this reprocessing step, recovered orbital parameters can deviate substantially from injected values or yield reduced detection significance. For V1298Tau b, >5σ detections of H2O, H2S, and CO are achievable within 10 hours ( 2 nights). For TOI-451c, distinguishing sub-solar from super-solar C/O ratios requires ≥18 hours. HRCCS with ELT/ANDES will therefore be a key tool for probing the atmospheric diversity of young, long-period sub-Neptunes in the ELT era.
papers.summary:
**Why It Matters**

Imagine trying to smell the aroma of a distant campfire while standing next to a busy highway—that's similar to the challenge astronomers face when studying the atmospheres of planets orbiting other stars. Most exoplanet atmosphere research has focused on scorching hot worlds that orbit incredibly close to their stars, but these represent just a fraction of the planetary diversity in our galaxy. The real prize is studying cooler, more distant planets that might be more representative of typical worlds—including young planets that are still evolving and could teach us about how planetary systems form. This research pushes the boundaries of what's possible, developing techniques to peer into the atmospheres of these elusive, Earth-sized to Neptune-sized worlds.

**What They Did**

The researchers used computer simulations to test whether a revolutionary new telescope system—the Extremely Large Telescope paired with an instrument called ANDES—could detect the chemical fingerprints of gases in the atmospheres of two specific planets: V1298Tau b and TOI-451c. These planets are roughly the size of Neptune but orbit much farther from their stars than the typical "hot" planets we usually study. The team simulated observations using a technique that looks for tiny shifts in light wavelengths as planets move toward and away from us during their orbits—like hearing the pitch change of an ambulance siren as it passes by. They tested different observation strategies and mathematical approaches to separate the faint planetary signals from interference caused by Earth's own atmosphere.

**Key Findings**

The simulations showed remarkably promising results. For V1298Tau b, the telescope system could detect water vapor, hydrogen sulfide, and carbon monoxide in just 10 hours of observation time spread over two nights—achieving detection confidence levels greater than 99.9999%. For the more challenging TOI-451c, the system could distinguish between different atmospheric compositions (specifically, whether carbon or oxygen is more abundant) with about 18 hours of observations. Crucially, the team discovered that including observations taken when the planet isn't crossing in front of its star dramatically improves detection success, but only if the data processing accounts for this properly.

**Looking Forward**

This research opens a new frontier in exoplanet science. When the Extremely Large Telescope begins operations later this decade, astronomers will finally be able to study the atmospheric chemistry of smaller, cooler planets that may be more similar to worlds in our own solar system. Understanding these young planetary atmospheres will help us piece together how planets form and evolve, potentially revealing whether the ingredients for life are common throughout the galaxy.
papers.summary_html:
Why It Matters

Imagine trying to smell the aroma of a distant campfire while standing next to a busy highway—that's similar to the challenge astronomers face when studying the atmospheres of planets orbiting other stars. Most exoplanet atmosphere research has focused on scorching hot worlds that orbit incredibly close to their stars, but these represent just a fraction of the planetary diversity in our galaxy. The real prize is studying cooler, more distant planets that might be more representative of typical worlds—including young planets that are still evolving and could teach us about how planetary systems form. This research pushes the boundaries of what's possible, developing techniques to peer into the atmospheres of these elusive, Earth-sized to Neptune-sized worlds.

What They Did

The researchers used computer simulations to test whether a revolutionary new telescope system—the Extremely Large Telescope paired with an instrument called ANDES—could detect the chemical fingerprints of gases in the atmospheres of two specific planets: V1298Tau b and TOI-451c. These planets are roughly the size of Neptune but orbit much farther from their stars than the typical "hot" planets we usually study. The team simulated observations using a technique that looks for tiny shifts in light wavelengths as planets move toward and away from us during their orbits—like hearing the pitch change of an ambulance siren as it passes by. They tested different observation strategies and mathematical approaches to separate the faint planetary signals from interference caused by Earth's own atmosphere.

Key Findings

The simulations showed remarkably promising results. For V1298Tau b, the telescope system could detect water vapor, hydrogen sulfide, and carbon monoxide in just 10 hours of observation time spread over two nights—achieving detection confidence levels greater than 99.9999%. For the more challenging TOI-451c, the system could distinguish between different atmospheric compositions (specifically, whether carbon or oxygen is more abundant) with about 18 hours of observations. Crucially, the team discovered that including observations taken when the planet isn't crossing in front of its star dramatically improves detection success, but only if the data processing accounts for this properly.

Looking Forward

This research opens a new frontier in exoplanet science. When the Extremely Large Telescope begins operations later this decade, astronomers will finally be able to study the atmospheric chemistry of smaller, cooler planets that may be more similar to worlds in our own solar system. Understanding these young planetary atmospheres will help us piece together how planets form and evolve, potentially revealing whether the ingredients for life are common throughout the galaxy.

tweet_hook.hook: 🔭 We're about to peek into the atmospheres of baby Neptune-like worlds for the first time!
tweet_hook.claim: New ultra-powerful telescopes will let us analyze the air around young, distant planets that are still forming - something we've never been able to do before.
tweet_hook.evidence: The ELT/ANDES telescope will use advanced light-filtering tech to study planets much smaller and farther out than current capabilities allow.
tweet_hook.question: What secrets might these planetary nurseries reveal about how worlds like ours form? 🪐

papers.title: KMT-2024-BLG-3237: Another Free-Floating Planet Candidate with Angular Einstein Radius Measurement
papers.abstract: Planet formation theories suggest the presence of free-floating planets (FFPs) that are ejected from their formation sites. While these planets emit very little light, they can be identified through gravitational microlensing. Here, we report the discovery of a FFP candidate in the microlensing event KMT-2024-BLG-3237. The observed light curve exhibits strong finite-source effects characterized by a small amplitude ( 0.9\, mag) and a short timescale ( 3\, days). The analysis yields an Einstein timescale of t E = 0.54±0.02\, days and an angular Einstein radius of θ E = 6.30±0.48\,μ as. The measurements make it possible to estimate the lens mass as M eq 102\,M⊕\,(π rₑl/16\,μ as)⁻¹, where π rₑl is the relative lens-source parallax. Depending on the unknown π rₑl, the lens could be a Neptune-mass planet (π rₑl eq 0.1\, mas) or a Saturn-mass planet (π rₑl eq 16\,μ as). A Bayesian analysis yields the lens mass M = 67.3₋₄₂.₅⁺¹⁰³.²\,M⊕ and the lens distance D L = 7.34₋₂.₁₁⁺⁰.⁹⁶\, kpc. This lens is the eleventh isolated microlens with a measurement of θ E < 10\,μ as. We find that additional searches for possible signatures of a lens host do not show significant evidence for the host.
papers.summary:
**Why It Matters**

Imagine planets wandering through space completely alone—no star to orbit, no warmth to sustain them, just drifting endlessly through the cosmic dark. These "rogue planets" were once theoretical outcasts, predicted by computer models but nearly impossible to find. Now, astronomers are discovering them using a clever detective technique, and each new find helps us understand how violent and chaotic planet formation can be. These cosmic orphans tell the story of planetary systems gone wrong, where gravitational battles ejected worlds into the void. Understanding how many rogue planets exist could revolutionize our picture of how common planets really are in our galaxy.

**What They Did**

The research team used a technique called gravitational microlensing, which works like a cosmic magnifying glass. When a massive object passes between Earth and a distant background star, the object's gravity bends and focuses the starlight, making the star appear brighter for a brief period. It's similar to how a glass lens can focus sunlight, except gravity does the focusing. The astronomers monitored millions of stars, waiting for these telltale brightening events. When they spotted one lasting just three days with very specific characteristics—particularly the way the brightness changed—they knew they had found something special: a planet with no host star.

**Key Findings**

The newly discovered rogue planet, designated KMT-2024-BLG-3237, weighs somewhere between 67 Earth masses (about twice as heavy as Neptune) and could be as massive as Saturn. It's located roughly 24,000 light-years away, drifting alone in the direction of our galaxy's center. The planet is completely dark and cold, receiving no energy from a nearby star. This discovery marks the eleventh isolated planet found using this ultra-precise measurement technique, adding to a growing census of cosmic wanderers.

**Looking Forward**

Each rogue planet discovery brings us closer to answering a fundamental question: just how many planets are out there? Some estimates suggest rogue planets might outnumber stars in our galaxy. Future space telescopes designed specifically for microlensing surveys could reveal thousands more, transforming our understanding of planetary formation and potentially showing us that planets—even lonely ones—are everywhere.

tweet_hook.hook: 🌍 Scientists just spotted a rogue planet floating alone in the darkness of space
tweet_hook.claim: This 'free-floating planet' was kicked out of its solar system and now wanders the galaxy by itself, detected only by how it bends light from distant stars
tweet_hook.evidence: The planet created a brief 3-day gravitational lensing event, acting like a cosmic magnifying glass
tweet_hook.question: How many orphaned worlds are drifting invisibly through our galaxy? 🔭

papers.title: Detection of circumstellar disks around nearby young brown dwarf candidates
papers.abstract: It is important to detect and study circumstellar disks around late-M and brown dwarfs of nearby young associations to understand how these very low-mass objects form and how rocky planets form around them. The detection of new very low-mass members of nearby young associations will also significantly improve our current understanding of young associations. We searched for new young very low-mass members with circumstellar disks in a sample of 3928 candidates. We constructed spectral energy distributions of all candidates using observational photometric data from DENIS, 2MASS, and WISE and trigonometric parallaxes from Gaia to detect infrared excess emission that indicates the presence of circumstellar disks. We then followed up spectroscopic observations of candidates to search for lithium to confirm their youth. The Hₐlpha emission line was used to detect accretion. We detected 23 among the 3928 candidates with circumstellar disks: Ten objects are new, and 13 were previously reported in the literature. Our mass estimates also indicate that 21 are brown dwarf candidates and 2 are very low-mass stars. DENIS J0534552-104808 has a Gaia distance of 238 pc and might be the first brown dwarf candidate member of a foreground population in front of the Orion D cloud. This foreground population is probably associated with the supergiant kappa Ori. Based on our spectroscopic observations, we detected lithium in 11 candidates. We also identified seven accretors and one potential accretor. The intense long-lived accretion detected in DENIS-P J0500245-333042, a 20 Myr old brown dwarf candidate may be additional evidence to favor the formation of rocky planets around very low-mass objects.
papers.summary:
**Why It Matters**

Brown dwarfs are fascinating cosmic oddities—they're too small to be stars but too big to be planets, earning them the nickname "failed stars." These dim objects might seem insignificant, but they could hold crucial clues about how planets form around the smallest, coolest objects in our universe. Understanding whether rocky planets like Earth can form around brown dwarfs would expand our ideas about where life might exist. Since brown dwarfs are incredibly common throughout the galaxy, discovering that they can host planetary systems would dramatically increase the number of potential worlds where life could take hold.

**What They Did**

The research team acted like cosmic detectives, searching through a database of nearly 4,000 suspicious objects to find brown dwarfs surrounded by disks of dust and gas—the raw materials from which planets form. They used multiple space telescopes to measure the brightness of these objects at different wavelengths of light, like taking their temperature from afar. When an object appeared brighter in infrared light than expected, it suggested warm dust swirling around it. The researchers then used ground-based telescopes to take detailed spectra—essentially breaking apart the light like a prism to reveal chemical fingerprints. They looked for lithium, which acts like a cosmic birth certificate since young objects haven't had time to destroy it yet.

**Key Findings**

The team discovered 23 brown dwarf candidates with circumstellar disks, including 10 completely new finds. Most exciting was their detection of ongoing accretion—material actively falling from the disk onto the brown dwarf—in several objects. One brown dwarf, despite being a relatively ancient 20 million years old, is still hungrily consuming material from its surrounding disk, suggesting these disks can persist much longer than scientists expected.

**Looking Forward**

These long-lived disks around brown dwarfs suggest that rocky planets might indeed form around these failed stars. This discovery could revolutionize our understanding of planetary formation and expand the definition of habitable systems throughout our galaxy.

tweet_hook.hook: Scientists just discovered something incredible about failed stars and planet formation! 🌟
tweet_hook.claim: Researchers found circumstellar disks around brown dwarfs - 'failed stars' that could still form rocky planets despite being too small to ignite nuclear fusion
tweet_hook.evidence: By analyzing 3,928 candidates, they're uncovering how planets might form around objects smaller than our Sun
tweet_hook.question: Could Earth-like worlds exist around stars that never truly became stars? 🪐

papers.title: JWST COMPASS: A NIRSpec G395H Transmission Spectrum of Radius Valley-Dweller TOI-260 b
papers.abstract: We present a JWST/NIRSpec G395H transmission spectrum of TOI-260 b, a T_eq 490 K, R_p = 1.76\,R_⊕ planet. The transmission spectrum is derived by combining two transit observations, collected as part of the JWST COMPASS program. We achieved the same median transit depth precision of 37 ppm in both visits, and a median precision of 26 ppm when combining the spectroscopic light curves from the two visits. Implementing a 30-pixel-wide (R 200) spectroscopic binning scheme, we find that the transmission spectrum is mostly featureless, with a possible feature around 3.17 μm. We assess the significance of any features in the transmission spectrum with a suite of non-parametric models, which confirm the presence of a potential feature in the NRS1 bandpass and an offset between the NRS1 and NRS2 detectors. To investigate the atmospheric composition of TOI-260 b, we run a series of PLATON retrievals. We do not detect any clear molecular signatures, but the combined data from the two visits are sufficient to constrain the atmospheric metallicity to greater than 200× solar, assuming no opaque deck 2.5 mbar. We also investigate causes of the potential feature near 3.17 μm; while we find some compatible gaseous species and cannot fully discard an astrophysical origin, we suspect a systematics origin due to the variance in strength and position of the feature. Overall, this look at TOI-260 b adds to the small sample of radius-valley planets, which already seem to show a diversity in their atmospheric compositions. Determining the true nature of these enigmatic planets will require a larger telescope time investment.
papers.summary:
**Why It Matters**

Imagine finding a planet that's bigger than Earth but smaller than Neptune—a world that sits right in the sweet spot astronomers call the "radius valley." These mysterious planets don't exist in our solar system, yet they're incredibly common throughout the galaxy. Scientists are racing to understand what these worlds are like because they might represent an entirely different class of planet, with atmospheres and compositions unlike anything we know. By studying TOI-260 b, a planet 1.76 times Earth's radius orbiting a distant star, researchers are helping solve one of the biggest puzzles in planetary science: what are these in-between worlds really made of?

**What They Did**

The team used the James Webb Space Telescope, humanity's most powerful space observatory, to watch TOI-260 b cross in front of its host star twice. Think of it like watching a shadow pass across a bright light—as the planet blocks some starlight, different colors of light are absorbed by its atmosphere. By carefully measuring which colors get dimmed and by how much, scientists can determine what gases exist in the planet's air. They focused on infrared light, which is invisible to our eyes but reveals the chemical fingerprints of atmospheric molecules. The observations were incredibly precise, detecting changes in brightness of just 37 parts per million—like noticing if a single lightbulb dimmed in a stadium of 27,000 lights.

**Key Findings**

The planet's atmosphere appears surprisingly bland. Instead of the clear chemical signatures scientists hoped to find, TOI-260 b's atmosphere seems mostly featureless, possibly shrouded in thick haze or clouds. However, the data revealed that if the planet does have a clear atmosphere, it must be extremely metal-rich—at least 200 times more metallic than our Sun. There was one intriguing possible feature detected around a specific wavelength, but researchers suspect this might be an instrumental quirk rather than a real atmospheric signal.

**Looking Forward**

This study adds another piece to the radius valley puzzle, showing these worlds have surprisingly diverse atmospheres. Some are clear, others are hazy, and each seems to tell a different story about how medium-sized planets form and evolve. Understanding these planets will require many more observations, but each new world studied brings us closer to understanding this mysterious class of planets that dominates our galaxy.

tweet_hook.hook: 🔭 JWST just peered into the atmosphere of a mysterious 'in-between' planet!
tweet_hook.claim: Scientists used the James Webb Space Telescope to study TOI-260 b, a planet 1.76x Earth's size that sits in the puzzling 'radius valley' between small rocky and large gassy worlds.
tweet_hook.evidence: They achieved incredible precision of just 26 ppm by combining two separate observations, revealing details about this 490K world's atmosphere.
tweet_hook.question: What secrets might this 'Goldilocks-sized' planet reveal about how worlds form? 🪐

papers.title: Eating planets makes you younger: The magnetic dynamo rejuvenation of GJ 504 by planetary engulfment
papers.abstract: With the discovery of a few thousand exoplanets, questions have been raised regarding star-planet interactions and whether the presence of a companion may affect stellar properties. GJ 504 is an evolved (2 Gyr) Sun-like star with a short rotation period (3.4 d) and an intense magnetic activity, which is in stark contrast with what would be expected at such an evolutionary stage. One possible explanation is that a close-in, Jupiter-mass planet was pushed starwards by the action of stellar tides, inducing a stellar spin-up and ultimately a rejuvenation of the stellar magnetic dynamo. By characterising the large-scale magnetic field and magnetised wind of GJ 504, we aim to provide additional observational constraints to test such scenario. We analysed spectropolarimetric observations of GJ 504 collected with ESPaDOnS. Using Zeeman-Doppler imaging, we found a large-scale, dipolar, non-axisymmetric magnetic field with an average strength of 5.3 G, similar to that of evolved early-G type stars. We fed the magnetic field information into our 3D MHD simulation of the stellar wind and space environment of GJ 504, from which we constrained the wind-driven angular momentum loss ( J). We then compared J to rotational evolutionary tracks of GJ 504 for two scenarios: evolution with and without the engulfment of a close-in, Jupiter-mass companion. Between the two scenarios, only the planet engulfment can explain the observational constraints obtained previously in the literature, such as the stellar rotation and X-ray luminosity, and the J we derived and rescaled to account for underestimated magnetic field strength. Although there are many other stars with similar masses and rotation periods whose rotation evolution does not require planet engulfment, we also identified HD 75332 as a candidate for planet engulfment, suggesting that GJ 504 may not be an isolated case.
papers.summary:
**Why It Matters**

Stars are supposed to slow down as they age, like spinning tops gradually losing momentum. But astronomers have discovered something puzzling: some old stars are spinning surprisingly fast and behaving like they're much younger than they should be. This mystery matters because it suggests dramatic events can completely reshape a star's life story—including the possibility that stars sometimes devour their own planets. Understanding these cosmic cannibalistic events helps us piece together how planetary systems evolve and survive, including what might happen to planets in our own solar system billions of years from now.

**What They Did**

The research team focused on a star called GJ 504, located about 57 light-years away. This star is roughly 2 billion years old (middle-aged by stellar standards) but spins once every 3.4 days—incredibly fast for its age. The researchers used a specialized instrument in Hawaii to study the star's magnetic field by analyzing how light from the star becomes polarized. Think of it like using special glasses to see the invisible magnetic forces surrounding the star. They then fed this magnetic field data into computer simulations to model how the star's magnetic field controls the flow of charged particles streaming away from it, similar to how our Sun creates the solar wind.

**Key Findings**

The star's magnetic field strength measures about 5.3 Gauss—roughly ten times stronger than Earth's magnetic field. Most importantly, the team's simulations revealed that only one scenario could explain all of GJ 504's unusual properties: the star must have swallowed a Jupiter-sized planet sometime in its past. This planetary meal would have spun up the star like a figure skater pulling in their arms, rejuvenating its magnetic activity and making it behave decades younger than its actual age.

**Looking Forward**

This research suggests that planetary engulfment might be more common than previously thought. The team identified another star, HD 75332, as a potential second case. As we discover more exoplanets, understanding these star-planet interactions becomes crucial for predicting which planetary systems will survive intact and which might experience such dramatic cosmic consumption events.

tweet_hook.hook: 🪐 BREAKING: Scientists discover a star that literally ate a planet and became younger! ⭐
tweet_hook.claim: GJ 504, a 2-billion-year-old star, is spinning fast and acting young because it devoured a Jupiter-sized planet. The planetary meal recharged its magnetic field like a cosmic energy drink.
tweet_hook.evidence: The star spins every 3.4 days (should take weeks) and shows intense magnetic activity that stars its age shouldn't have
tweet_hook.question: What other stars might be hiding evidence of their planetary snacks? 🤔

papers.title: Warm Debris Disk Candidates around Nearby FGK Stars from LAMOST DR12
papers.abstract: Warm debris disks around main-sequence stars trace late-stage terrestrial planet formation. Motivated by the need for systematic searches of such systems, we identify debris disk candidates around FGK stars within 150 pc by combining a spectroscopically selected sample from LAMOST DR12 with Gaia astrometry and multi-band infrared photometry. Infrared excesses are identified through SED fitting and validated using conservative, source-by-source checks. This approach yields a final sample of 12 debris disk candidates including ten new detections. Stellar age research indicate that most of the host stars are several billion years old. NEOWISE monitoring reveals no significant W1/W2 variability, consistent with a circumstellar origin of the infrared excess. while a search for co-moving companions using Gaia DR3 reveals possible companions for only two candidates at very large projected separations ( 10⁴ au). Three candidates exhibit excess emission in both the W3 and W4 bands, allowing estimates of characteristic dust properties. This work establishes a small yet reliable sample of debris disk candidates anchored in homogeneous LAMOST spectroscopy, providing a foundation for future studies of debris disk evolution and stellar activity.
papers.summary:
**Why It Matters**

Imagine the aftermath of a cosmic construction project—dust and debris scattered around a star where rocky planets like Earth are still forming or have recently formed. These "debris disks" are like celestial breadcrumbs that help astronomers understand how planetary systems evolve over billions of years. By studying these dusty rings around other stars, scientists can piece together the violent, chaotic process that created our own solar system. This research is particularly exciting because it focuses on "warm" debris disks around stars similar to our Sun, offering clues about how Earth-like worlds come to be and what our solar system might have looked like in its youth.

**What They Did**

The research team acted like cosmic detectives, sifting through data from multiple space surveys to find hidden treasure. They started with information from LAMOST, a Chinese telescope that analyzes the light from hundreds of thousands of stars to determine their properties. Then they combined this with precise distance measurements from the European Gaia spacecraft and infrared observations that can detect the warm glow of dust around stars. Think of it like using multiple filters to pan for gold—each dataset helped them eliminate false alarms and focus on genuine debris disk candidates. They specifically looked at stars similar to our Sun within 150 light-years of Earth, close enough for detailed follow-up studies.

**Key Findings**

The team discovered 12 debris disk candidates, with 10 being completely new finds—like uncovering a dozen previously unknown archaeological sites. Most of these dusty systems orbit stars that are several billion years old, suggesting we're seeing the late stages of planet formation when rocky worlds have mostly finished assembling. Importantly, the dust showed steady infrared glow rather than flickering, confirming it truly surrounds these stars rather than coming from background sources.

**Looking Forward**

This catalog provides astronomers with prime targets for future observations using advanced telescopes, potentially revealing the birth pangs of distant Earth-like worlds and helping us understand our place in the cosmic story of planet formation.

tweet_hook.hook: 🌟 Scientists just found cosmic 'construction sites' where Earth-like planets might be forming!
tweet_hook.claim: Researchers discovered 12 nearby star systems with warm debris disks - the telltale sign that rocky planets like Earth could be assembling from leftover space dust and rocks.
tweet_hook.evidence: These systems are all within 150 light-years of Earth, making them prime targets for studying how terrestrial planets form.
tweet_hook.question: Could we be witnessing the birth of future Earths in our cosmic neighborhood? 🌍✨

papers.title: TEPCat: The Transiting Extrasolar Planet Catalogue
papers.abstract: Transiting extrasolar planets are extraordinarily valuable for understanding the characteristics and formation of planets, because they are the only exoplanets whose physical and orbital properties can be measured to high precision. Thousands are now known, and it is important to maintain a database of them for use by the scientific community. TEPCat performs this task: it is a critical compilation of the physical and observable properties of the known transiting planetary systems. This work introduces the motivation for TEPCat, its scope, contents, and implementation. Example plots of interesting quantities are constructed. The classification of planets and of the eclipse features in their light curves is discussed. TEPCat is maintained and freely available online at https://www.astro.keele.ac.uk/jkt/tepcat/
papers.summary:
**Why It Matters**

Imagine trying to study a distant city by only observing the lights in its windows—that's essentially what astronomers face when studying planets orbiting other stars. Most of the thousands of exoplanets we've discovered are invisible dots of light next to their blazingly bright parent stars. But a special group of these alien worlds occasionally pass directly in front of their stars as seen from Earth, creating tiny eclipses that reveal incredible secrets about their size, mass, atmosphere, and composition. These "transiting" planets are astronomical goldmines, offering our best chance to truly understand what other worlds are like and how planetary systems form and evolve throughout the universe.

**What They Did**

The researchers created something like a comprehensive phone book for transiting exoplanets—a detailed online database called TEPCat that catalogs every known planet that eclipses its star. Just as you might organize your music collection by artist, album, and genre, they systematically gathered and organized thousands of measurements about each planet system: how big the planets are, how much they weigh, how close they orbit to their stars, what their atmospheres might contain, and dozens of other crucial characteristics. They also classified different types of eclipse patterns in the brightness measurements, creating a standardized system that astronomers worldwide can use to compare planets and identify the most interesting targets for further study.

**Key Findings**

While this paper primarily introduces the database rather than announcing new discoveries, TEPCat reveals fascinating patterns across the thousands of transiting worlds now catalogued. The database encompasses planets ranging from scorching hot gas giants larger than Jupiter that complete their orbits in just days, to small rocky worlds that might harbor conditions suitable for life. By organizing all this information in one accessible place, the catalogue enables scientists to spot trends and relationships that would be impossible to see when studying planets individually—like noticing that certain types of stars tend to host particular kinds of planetary systems.

**Looking Forward**

TEPCat serves as mission control for the golden age of exoplanet discovery we're entering. With powerful new telescopes like the James Webb Space Telescope now operational and future missions being planned, astronomers will soon be able to analyze the atmospheres of Earth-like worlds in unprecedented detail. This database ensures that scientists can quickly identify the most promising candidates for these detailed studies, potentially leading us to worlds with signs of water, oxygen, or even life itself.

tweet_hook.hook: 🪐 THOUSANDS of alien worlds are hiding in plain sight—and we're cataloging their secrets
tweet_hook.claim: Scientists have discovered thousands of exoplanets that pass directly in front of their stars, giving us unprecedented views into alien worlds' true nature
tweet_hook.evidence: These 'transiting' planets are the ONLY ones where we can measure exact size, mass, and orbital details with incredible precision
tweet_hook.question: What mind-blowing discoveries await in this treasure trove of planetary data? 🔭✨
